definition: A legal doctrine holding that when speech is merely a secondary part of regulated non-speech activity (conduct), the government can regulate it without triggering full First Amendment scrutiny. The key question is whether there is separate non-speech conduct being regulated.
examples: Colorado argued therapy is regulated conduct and speech is just the tool used to deliver it, like a scalpel in surgery. Petitioner countered that where treatment consists only of speech, the doctrine does not apply.
